name: Heavener Runestone
description: The Heavener Runestone is a large stone located in Heavener, Oklahoma, bearing inscriptions that some believe are Norse runes. Proponents suggest the stone provides evidence of pre-Columbian Norse exploration of North America, potentially dating back as early as the 6th century AD. The stone was first documented in the 1830s, and its 'discovery' is attributed to Choctaw Indians. The inscriptions consist of several characters that some interpret as runes, specifically representing the letters 'G', 'N', 'O', 'M', and 'E', or possibly a longer phrase if read in a different sequence or through cryptographic means. The primary argument in favor of the Norse origin is the perceived similarity of the inscriptions to Elder Futhark runes. However, mainstream archaeologists and runologists generally dispute this interpretation. They argue that the inscriptions are more likely the result of natural weathering, deliberate vandalism, or, at best, a poorly executed attempt to copy Norse runes by someone unfamiliar with the language and writing system. The stone's location, far inland from any plausible Norse landing site, also poses a logistical challenge to the Norse-contact hypothesis. Furthermore, no other substantial archaeological evidence of a Norse presence has been found in the area, despite extensive searches. The lack of a clear and universally accepted translation, and the possibility of later origin, undermine the claims of pre-Columbian Norse presence. The stone remains a local landmark and a subject of ongoing debate between proponents of alternative history and mainstream academics.
subtitle: Oklahoma stone with alleged Norse runes

claims.claim: The Heavener Runestone proves pre-Columbian Norse presence in Oklahoma.
claims.evidence: The inscriptions resemble Elder Futhark runes.
claims.criticism: Runes are ambiguous, could be natural formations, vandalized, or misinterpreted. No supporting archaeological evidence.

claims.claim: The inscription contains a hidden message, possibly a name or date.
claims.evidence: Proponents offer various interpretations of the runes, including cryptographic analyses.
claims.criticism: Interpretations are subjective and lack scholarly consensus. The 'runes' do not form a coherent sentence in any known Norse language.

claims.claim: The stone was intentionally placed at its location by Norse explorers.
claims.evidence: The stone's size and prominent position suggest deliberate placement.
claims.criticism: Geological processes could explain the stone's location. No evidence of Norse tools or methods of transportation were found nearby.

keyFigures.name: Gloria Farley
keyFigures.role: Promoted Norse origin theory, researched and documented runestones.

keyFigures.name: Richard Nielsen
keyFigures.role: Offered interpretations of the runic inscriptions.

keyFigures.name: Henrik Williams
keyFigures.role: Runologist who has critiqued the Norse interpretation.

timeline.event: Stone is first documented by Europeans.
Interest in the stone's origin grows.
Gloria Farley begins her research and promotion of the Norse theory.
Debate continues between proponents and mainstream scholars.

sources.author: Farley, Gloria Stewart
sources.work: In Plain Sight: Old World Records in Ancient America
sources.title: Farley, Gloria Stewart

sources.author: Feder, Kenneth L.
sources.work: Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology
sources.title: Feder, Kenneth L.
